steps.title: Host environment
steps.hint: Where you sit and run your editor, terminal, and local builds.

steps.title: Target output
steps.hint: What you ship or run when the project is done.

steps.title: Primary language
steps.hint: Narrows toolchain families. Choose the closest match.

steps.title: Toolchain family
steps.hint: Compiler and linker stack. Leave as “help me choose” if unsure.

steps.title: Build & run model
steps.hint: Where compilation happens vs where the binary runs.

options.label: Build and run on the host OS
Build inside WSL; artifacts for Windows or Linux
Cross-compile on host (e.g. mingw on Linux)
Local edit only; CI builds releases

recommendations.title: Cross-compile Windows binaries from Linux
recommendations.summary: Use a MinGW-w64 cross toolchain on Linux (distro package or LLVM-MinGW). Validate with Wine only for smoke tests; use a Windows VM or CI for real QA.
tooling: GitHub Actions windows-latest for release builds
caveats: Not all Windows APIs behave correctly under Wine

recommendations.title: Windows C++ on a Windows + WSL2 machine
recommendations.summary: For shipping Windows binaries, prefer native MSVC or MinGW on Windows unless you have a strong Linux-only workflow. Use WSL2 when you need Linux build scripts, apt packages, or identical CI to Linux servers—but install a Windows-targeting toolchain inside WSL (mingw-w64) or build natively on Windows for fewer path surprises.
tooling: Native: Visual Studio Build Tools + MSVC
WSL: sudo apt install mingw-w64; cmake -DCMAKE_SYSTEM_NAME=Windows
caveats: Mixing \ and / paths across WSL and Windows confuses some tools
Debugging GUI apps across WSL boundary is awkward—use native Windows toolchain for UI work

recommendations.title: Native Windows C++ with MSVC
recommendations.summary: Install Visual Studio Build Tools or full Visual Studio with the “Desktop development with C++” workload. This is the default for Win32, WinUI, and most third-party Windows libraries.
caveats: MSVC object files are not link-compatible with MinGW—pick one family per project

recommendations.title: Native Windows C++ with MinGW-w64
recommendations.summary: Install MSYS2 and the mingw-w64 toolchain. Good for open-source stacks, GCC-only dependencies, and avoiding the full Visual Studio install.
caveats: Some proprietary libs ship MSVC import libs only
Prefer UCRT toolchain over legacy mingw32 unless you know you need it

recommendations.title: Linux targets from Windows via WSL2
recommendations.summary: Install your distro in WSL2 and use normal Linux packages (build-essential, rustup, etc.). Keep the repo on the Linux filesystem (/home/...) for best I/O; access from Windows via \\wsl$\ when needed.
caveats: Avoid building Linux binaries on /mnt/c/—slow and permission quirks
GUI apps need WSLg or an X server

recommendations.title: .NET on Windows
recommendations.summary: Install the .NET SDK for your target band. Use native Windows for WinUI and tight Visual Studio integration; WSL is fine for ASP.NET and cross-platform libraries.
tooling: Optional: WSL for Linux-target builds (dotnet publish -r linux-x64)
caveats: WinUI 3 projects generally require Windows-native tooling

recommendations.title: Rust cross-compilation
recommendations.summary: Use rustup targets (rustup target add x86_64-pc-windows-msvc or x86_64-pc-windows-gnu). MSVC target needs the Windows SDK on Windows; GNU target needs mingw on Linux/macOS.
caveats: msvc vs gnu ABI choice matters for linking C dependencies

recommendations.title: Web applications
recommendations.summary: Host OS matters less than runtime choice. Use Node LTS or your framework’s documented stack; containerize for parity with production.
caveats: Match Node version to deployment target

recommendations.title: General guidance
recommendations.summary: Complete each step above. DevCentr ranks recommendations by how many of your choices match known patterns. When in doubt: build on the same OS family as your target, or use the platform’s official SDK (MSVC for Windows desktop, Xcode for macOS, NDK for Android).
tooling: Document your choice in the repo README
caveats: Cross-compilation saves dual-booting but adds debugging cost
